missions.goal: find out if Leto is planning to strike first
secure spice supplies before we run short
our biomass stores are dangerously low — find a source, any source

narrativeContexts.narrative: Leto named the terms plainly. No theater, no pretense of generosity. A household servant understood the offer and what refusing it would cost.
"I don't believe in altruism." Feyd handed over the payment anyway. A household servant accepted it without commenting on the contradiction.
Supply and demand. Irulan understood both. The transaction was neither generous nor cruel. It was correct.
Hasimir explained the scheme over wine. The wine was better than the ethics. He drank both.
Mohiam never paid directly. There was always a gift, a loan, a favor returned — something that required context to understand as what it was.
Tyros stood at the window. Nothing required her attention. The inattention itself was a kind of attention — she was watching the gaps between events.
Thufir signed without pleasure. Wealth was a tool, not a reward. The tool was being applied correctly.
The table was full. Ahsir sat at its head and calculated the cost of every face present.
"I don't believe in altruism." Iakin handed over the payment anyway. A household servant accepted it without commenting on the contradiction.
The report was complete and correct and Paul felt nothing about it. That was the professional standard. He had met it.
Leto transferred the resource without ceremony. It was useful to he. That was the only criterion that had been applied.
Irulan spoke carefully, placing each word like a stone in a wall. A superior could accept the wall or watch it fall.
The order went through three people before reaching a subordinate. Vladimir didn't trust single points of failure.
Shaddam gave the order twice, rephrasing the second time to ensure the wording couldn't be exploited. Shaddam checked the door. Twice.
Thufir delivered the report. No analysis. No recommendation. Just the data, laid out with the care of someone who understood that the recipient would hear what they wanted to hear.
Vladimir spread the assets quietly — no single concentration large enough to be noticed, no single loss large enough to be catastrophic. The paranoia was structural.
The information changed several things. Duncan catalogued them quietly, making no indication of which one mattered most.
Gurney stood at the window. Nothing required his attention. The inattention itself was a kind of attention — he was watching the gaps between events.
Leto described the future as if it had already happened. In Atreides, the difference between prediction and plan was mostly timing.
Irulan shared what she knew. Not everything — enough to be useful, not enough to be dangerous. Shaddam recognized the calibration.
Supply and demand. Jessica understood both. The transaction was neither generous nor cruel. It was correct.
Vladimir waited. Not patiently — he had simply run out of things to control. The stillness was strategic.
Shaddam gave the compliment and watched Irulan's face for the telltale flicker — the faint confusion of someone who hadn't expected it. Irulan passed. Shaddam checked the door. Twice.
Edric had stopped calling it ambition. This was older than that — a ledger entry that had been accumulating interest for years.
Mohiam paid in small amounts through multiple channels. No single transaction would draw attention. The pattern would, but by then it wouldn't matter. Mohiam hadn't smiled in days.
Thufir did not smile. Scheming was not pleasure. It was architecture — and he was building something he would live inside without knowing it.
Kallig outlined the arrangement with the care of someone presenting a legal brief. The legality was not the point.
The training yard was empty except for two people who trusted each other enough to fight without rules. Duncan struck low. Paul blocked late. Neither acknowledged it.
Vladimir never paid directly. There was always a gift, a loan, a favor returned — something that required context to understand as what it was.
The feast was excellent. Shaddam tasted nothing. He was too busy watching who sat near whom and what that meant.

The money moved. The information followed. Edric and a household servant were past pretending this was anything other than what it was. Edric performed the motions. The feeling behind them was somewhere else.
Mohiam reviewed the accounts alone. The numbers were correct. That was either reassuring or the first sign that something was wrong.
The plan was meticulous. The feeling behind it was not. Rabban was owed something, and this was how the debt would be collected. Rabban hadn't smiled in days.
"An investment in the future." Armand said it the way people say things they don't quite believe. The money moved regardless.
Kasha smiled at every toast. By the third hour, the smile had become structural — load-bearing, involuntary, entirely effective.
The ceremony proceeded correctly. Dmitra observed every form. The guests would remember the occasion; she would remember what was said in the margins of it.
The balance sheet did not care about sentiment. Casy studied it anyway, looking for the gap between what was recorded and what was true.
Hasimir gave. He received. The transaction had a subtext that both parties read clearly and neither would ever mention.
Vladimir offered the gift and noted, with quiet precision, that he had accepted. That mattered. The nature of the gift mattered less than the acceptance.
